data.title: West Pharmaceutical signals 2026 revenue of $3.295B-$3.35B and adjusted EPS of $8.40-$8.75 driven by HVP components momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said “the year is off to a strong start” and reported “first quarter revenues of $845 million” and “adjusted EPS came in at $2.13,” adding that “due to our strong first quarter performance and the expected ongoing momentum in our business, we are increasing our revenue and adjusted EPS guidance for full year 2026.”
  • Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) positioned HVP components as the key driver, stating “HVP components grew 23% on an organic basis,” with “strong performance in both GLP-1 and non-GLP-1 revenues,” and that GLP-1 “contributed 10% of total company sales, consistent with the previous quarter.”
  • Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) linked outperformance to manufacturing execution, describing “operational excellence” actions including accelerated onboarding, “qualify second sites,” and “transfer of knowledge and implementation of best practices.”
  • Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said Q1 “exceed[ed] our expectations,” noting “price contributed 3.5 percentage points of growth,” and highlighted capital return: “the Board of Directors authorized a new $1 billion share repurchase program,” and “we repurchased 1.2 million shares for $298 million.”

Outlook

  • Management raised full-year 2026 guidance to “revenue to be in the range of $3.295 billion to $3.35 billion” and “adjusted earnings per share to be between $8.40 to $8.75.”
  • Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said the outlook assumes continued mix benefits and cost offsets while staying cautious: “The macro environment continues to be dynamic, and so we will remain prudent with our forecasting given we have 3 quarters to go in the year.”
  • Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) reiterated portfolio and deal assumptions: “We continue to expect to close the SmartDose transaction midyear,” and said the company expects “a net impact of single-digit millions after the mitigation efforts” from “rising oil and commodity prices.”
  • For Q2, Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) guided “revenue to be in the range of $830 million to $850 million” and “second quarter adjusted diluted earnings per share in the range of $2.05 to $2.12.”

Financial Results

  • West reported Q1 “revenues of $845 million,” “total company gross margin was 35.1%,” and “adjusted operating margins of 21.4%,” with Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) attributing the gross margin increase to “the positive mix shift of HVP components and price contribution.”
  • Segment detail included HVP components “$409 million,” HVP delivery devices “$124 million,” Standard Products “$161 million,” and West Vantage “$151 million,” as Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) described performance as “broad-based.”
  • Cash and spending items included “operating cash flow of $90 million,” “capital expenditures were $43 million,” and quarter-end cash of “$521 million,” while capex remained expected at “$250 million to $275 million for the year.”

Q&A

  • Patrick Donnelly, Citigroup Inc.: Asked what drove non-GLP-1 acceleration and how much was Annex 1 vs. Biologics/Biosimilars; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) replied growth was “mostly on already commercialized drugs,” and said Annex 1 is “up 66% over the first quarter of last year,” while Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) added, “If you recall, in Q4, we talked about demand outstripping supply.”
  • Patrick Donnelly, Citigroup Inc.: Asked about margin drivers and cadence; Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said “mix shift certainly does help” and added “we're expecting actually in the second half of the year is an extension or an expansion of our margins.”
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities: Asked about pull-forward ordering tied to Middle East conflict; Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said “we did not see any pull forward.”
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities: Asked about mitigation tools (hedges/price/supply chain); Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said, “we have multiple tools at our disposal,” and called it “premature to kind of explain all the details.”
  • Paul Knight, KeyBanc: Asked about bottlenecks and site-to-site customer qualification time; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said it can take “anywhere between 6 to 12 months.”
  • Kallum Titchmarsh, Morgan Stanley: Asked about Annex 1 duration and TAM capture; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said, “We've identified at least 6 billion units that are targeted to be converted,” and “The growth that we believe that we will continue to deliver on is 200 basis points per annum for multiple years.”
  • Kallum Titchmarsh, Morgan Stanley: Asked about APAC demand; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) cited increased activity in “biologics or the Biosimilar space” and “an increase of CDMOs, small biotech firms.”
  • Justin Bowers, Deutsche Bank: Asked about Dublin and West Coast manufacturing space demand and GLP-1 indications; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said Dublin CGM work is “finishing up at the end of second quarter,” and added GLP-1 has “several projects in the pipeline.”
  • Justin Bowers, Deutsche Bank: Asked if delivery device strength included incentives; Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said “it was all volume” and “There weren't any incentive payments.”
  • Daniel Markowitz, Evercore ISI: Asked what drove NovaPure strength; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said it was “driven by market demand of commercialized molecules in market already.”
  • Daniel Markowitz, Evercore ISI: Asked why full-year guide appears more first-half weighted; Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) cited “the roll-off of the CGM contract in the back half of the year,” calling it “about a $40 million headwind in the second half of the year.”
  • David Windley, Jefferies: Asked about sequential margin down and potential drags; Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said March was stronger than January as people ramped, adding “SmartDose did have some impact on that.”
  • David Windley, Jefferies: Asked about Eric Green’s retirement; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said, “as I think about the successor coming in, when appointed, we'll be in a very good position.”
  • Lawrence Solow, CJS Securities: Asked CEO search timing; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said his successor is expected “in the second half of this year.”
  • Lawrence Solow, CJS Securities: Asked where Annex 1 upgrades land on the HVP spectrum; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said, “it really depends on the customer and the molecule itself.”
  • Lawrence Solow, CJS Securities: Asked West Vantage cadence and margins; Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said Q3 could be “the trough,” drug handling is “$20 million of incremental revenue,” and drug handling margin is “at least twice as much on a gross margin basis as our current business.”
  • Brendan Smith, TD Cowen: Asked if Annex 1 upside could come from U.S./Asia; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said customers are making “more global decisions,” and Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) pointed to onshoring as a “potential accelerant.”
  • Brendan Smith, TD Cowen: Asked cadence of operational excellence rollout; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said “we're live right now,” and Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) said, “we have baked some of that operational improvement in the forecast.”
  • Tom DeBourcy, Nephron Research: Asked about delivery devices post SmartDose divestiture; Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) said, “we believe delivery devices is natural in this area as primary containment is a natural extension.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive, with questions pressing on sustainability (capacity, margins, pull-forward risk, back-half deceleration) but framed with compliments such as “Congrats on the results” and “Great print.”
  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks, using language such as Eric Green’s “growth strategy is delivering,” and remained mostly steady in Q&A, while declining to detail some mitigations as “premature to kind of explain all the details.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1 commentary sounded more confident on demand and execution, aligning with the raised full-year revenue and adjusted EPS guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance shifted up versus Q4’s initial 2026 framework: Q1 raised organic growth to “7% to 9%” from “5% to 7%,” and adjusted EPS to “$8.40 to $8.75” from “$7.85 to $8.20.”
  • Prepared remarks and Q&A focus moved from establishing conservatism around GLP-1/orals in Q4 to emphasizing Q1 broad-based outperformance, faster capacity ramp, and operational excellence, with Q1 also introducing a new “$1 billion share repurchase program.”
  • Analyst emphasis evolved from Q4’s heavy GLP-1 conservatism debate to Q1’s questions on non-GLP-1 acceleration, margin cadence, conflict-related ordering behavior, and the timing and economics of the West Vantage transition.
  • Management tone appeared more assured in Q1 as Bob McMahon described HVP ramping “faster than planned,” compared with Q4’s repeated emphasis on being “prudent” to start the year.

Risks and Concerns

  • Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) cited external cost pressure, saying “we've also incorporated rising oil and commodity prices,” with mitigation expected to limit exposure to “a net impact of single-digit millions.”
  • West expects portfolio noise from transactions and program roll-offs, as Robert McMahon (Senior VP & CFO) reiterated the company expects to close SmartDose “midyear” and cited the CGM roll-off as “about a $40 million headwind in the second half of the year.”
  • Capacity and qualification lead times remained a recurring constraint theme, with Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) noting second-site qualification can take “6 to 12 months.”
  • Leadership transition became a new uncertainty item, with Eric Green (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO) stating his successor is expected to be appointed “in the second half of this year.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a broad-based start to 2026, led by HVP components in both GLP-1 and non-GLP-1, supported by operational execution in Europe and ongoing Annex 1 conversion activity. The company raised full-year 2026 revenue and adjusted EPS guidance, maintained expectations for a midyear SmartDose transaction close, and outlined a second-half headwind from the CGM contract roll-off alongside mitigation plans for higher oil and commodity costs. Management also disclosed a new $1B share repurchase authorization and said a CEO successor is expected to be appointed in the second half of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Janus Henderson continues to be overweight international markets
data.description:
Michael Contopoulos, head of multi-asset macro investing at Janus Henderson Investors, is maintaining an overweight position on international markets, citing a growing valuation gap between the U.S. and the rest of the world.

In an interview with CNBC, Contopoulos expressed skepticism about the recent U.S. market rally, noting that Janus Henderson has held this international overweight stance since late 2025 and continues to see more opportunity abroad.

The strategist raised concerns about the narrow breadth of the S&P 500’s (SP500) recent 7% surge in April.

“The one thing that concerns me about that move within the U.S. is the narrowness of the move,” Contopoulos said. “You’re really seeing it in the highflyers, the sort of more liquidity-driven areas of the market. You don’t have the breadth yet.”

Addressing the apparent disconnect between bond (AGG), (BND), (VCIT), (MUB), (HYG), and stock market (SP500), (DJI), (COMP:IND) signals, Contopoulos argued that the two are not necessarily at odds.

He pointed to strong economic indicators, including jobless claims, retail sales, and first-quarter earnings growth, as evidence that robust growth naturally supports higher bond prices.

Fears around inflation driven by elevated oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) and secular changes are being reflected in bond markets, while stocks respond to the underlying economic strength.

The impact of the Iran war on international markets hinges largely on oil prices, according to Contopoulos. He warned that prices at $100 or above for four to six months would create problems for parts of Europe (VGK), (EZU), (BBEU), (IEUR), and emerging markets (IEMG), (VWO), (EEM), though commodity-producing nations could benefit.

However, if oil prices settle in the $80 to $90 range, “Europe could be okay, and earnings growth could follow suit,” he said.

Contopoulos emphasized that global economic conditions were strong heading into the conflict, with earnings growth accelerating in Europe, Japan (EWJ), and most emerging markets outside of China (EMXC), (KEMX).

“So far, we haven’t actually seen much data to suggest that that is going to roll over yet,” he noted, adding that the valuation gap between the U.S. and international markets “could shrink if you don’t have egregiously high oil prices for a long period of time.”

The firm’s commitment to international positioning remains firm.

“We’ve been overweight international markets now, really, since October or November 2025, and we continue to maintain that positioning,” Contopoulos confirmed.

Here are the top international market ETFs.

data.title: Lam Research's 'strong' Q3 results, outlook see positive views from analysts
data.description:
Shares of Lam Research (LRCX) dipped about 1% on Thursday despite third-quarter results and outlooks seeing positive reactions from analysts.

Needham kept its Buy rating and $300 price target on the chip equipment maker's stock.

"Lam Research reported strong results across-the-board, with C1Q26 revenue of $5.8B (+9% QoQ) above guidance mid-point. C2Q26 revenue was guided to $6.6B (+13% QoQ and well above most expectations), with gross margins expected at a record level of 50.5%. The company raise its 2026 WFE outlook to "$140B with bias to the upside" (from $135B prior), citing improvements in all end markets over the quarter. Although the buy-side number has been well above the sell-side and more or less here, this is still a remarkable beat," said analysts led by Charles Shi.

Morgan Stanley maintained its Equal-weight rating but increased the price target on the stock to $293 from $260.

"Despite softer NAND shipments, LAM continues to beat and raise, reflecting its evolution into a more diversified company with meaningful share gains in Logic and DRAM," said analysts led by Shane Brett.

The analysts noted that ASML (ASML) delivered a June quarter revenue guidance of 10% above Street, and Lam has now followed suit, both pointing to substantial upside to Morgan Stanley's 23% 2026 Wafer Fabrication Equipment, or WFE, growth forecast.

"LAM’s MarQ DRAM revenue of $1bn was 30% above its prior peak in DecQ25, and while we do think LAM is benefiting from more idiosyncratic tailwinds in the back-end — driving us to raise our CY26 growth forecast from 41% to 68% — we also see significant upside risk to our CY26 DRAM WFE forecast of $41.7bn (+33% y/y), with our early expectation now closer to $47.1bn (+50% y/y). Our view on DRAM capacity growth has been consistent: where there is a will, there is a way. However, the pace at which DRAM WFE is being pulled forward is both surprising and unprecedented," said Brett and his team.

However, the analysts added Lam's NAND shipments have now disappointed for a second consecutive quarter (March quarter: $447M versus Morgan Stanley's estimate of $627M), reflecting both capital discipline among its customers and Lam's evolution into a more diversified company with meaningful share gains in Logic and DRAM.

BNP Paribas kept its Neutral rating but increased the price target on the stock to $260 from $250.

"The stronger results echo the strong demand environment we have heard from end customers and equipment peers. We remain neutral given the elevated valuation and CY27 WFE expectations already discounting +21% y/y growth," said analysts Karl Ackerman and David O’Connor.

Related stocks: KLA (KLAC) and Applied Materials (AMAT) each rose about 1%, while ASML (ASML) dipped about 1% on Thursday.

Update: The story was updated with a BNP note and stock price movement.

data.title: American Express reaffirms 2026 guidance of 9%-10% revenue growth and $17.30-$17.90 EPS while increasing marketing and technology investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Express (AXP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We had a very strong start to the year. Revenue in the quarter grew 11% or 10% on an FX-adjusted basis, and EPS was $4.28, up 18% over the prior year." (Chairman & CEO Stephen Squeri)
  • "Based on our strong results to date and our confidence going forward, we've decided to increase our investments in marketing and technology to capitalize on key growth opportunities and build on our momentum." (Chairman & CEO Stephen Squeri)
  • "In March, we announced a multi-year global partnership with the NFL, making American Express the League's Official Payments Partner beginning with the 2026 season." (Chairman & CEO Stephen Squeri)
  • "In the quarter, we announced a road map for a series of commercial products and solutions that we're planning to roll out in the U.S. In 2026 for businesses of all sizes, starting with the launch of our new Graphite Business Cash Unlimited Card." (Chairman & CEO Stephen Squeri)
  • "Earlier this month, we introduced the Amex Agentic Commerce Experiences or ACE Developer Kit, which will enable the integration of American Express Cards into AI-powered transactions with trust and control." (Chairman & CEO Stephen Squeri)
  • "Q1 was a very good quarter. Revenue growth accelerated to 11% or 10% FX adjusted, with broad-based growth across revenue lines." (Chief Financial Officer Christophe Le Caillec)

Outlook

  • "Looking ahead, we're reaffirming our full year 2026 guidance of 9% to 10% annual revenue growth and EPS of $17.30 to $17.90." (Chairman & CEO Stephen Squeri)
  • "We are seeing stronger earnings than expected, and we have decided to increase investments in marketing and technology." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)
  • "We now expect marketing expenses to grow in the mid-single digits for the full year." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management repeated the same full-year 2026 ranges (revenue growth 9% to 10%, EPS $17.30 to $17.90) while adding that the Q1 outperformance created capacity to increase marketing and technology investment. "We're taking the over-delivery from an EPS perspective and investing that back into the business." (Chairman & CEO Squeri)

Financial Results

  • "Card Member spending grew 10% on a reported basis, and this is the highest quarterly growth in 3 years, driven by strong growth across both Goods & Services and T&E." (Chairman & CEO Squeri)
  • "Spend growth was about 1 percentage point higher than Q4, driven by T&E spending, up 9% FX adjusted, while Goods & Services growth remained stable, up 8% FX adjusted." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)
  • "Overall provision expense of $1.3 billion, included a reserve release of $24 million." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)
  • "Net card fees continued to be our fastest-growing line, up 16% FX adjusted, in line with Q4." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)
  • "Marketing spend was $1.5 billion this quarter, flat to last year." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)
  • "We returned $2.3 billion of capital to our shareholders including $0.7 billion of dividends and $1.7 billion of share repurchases." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)

Q&A

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: asked whether momentum could put 10% revenue growth “on the table” and what drove incremental marketing/tech; Chairman & CEO Squeri: "we've just delivered a quarter of 11%. So you can make your own judgment on that" and "when you have an over-delivery like we just had in the first quarter, it gives us that confidence that we can move those ROI thresholds down"; Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec: "there were also a few unexpected items on operating expenses" including "a court decision regarding VAT in Europe" and "a gain... as we completed the acquisition of the half of the joint venture we had in Switzerland."
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: pressed on airline softness and fuel impacts; Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec: "we did see airline growth softened in the last few weeks of March and into April, driven by travel disruptions from the Middle East conflict" and "the impact is not that large"; he added "fuel is less than 2% of the overall billed business."
  • Donald Fandetti, Wells Fargo: asked about confidence in expense management for middle market SMEs; Chairman & CEO Squeri: "in the next few months, we'll relaunch or launch Center" and "we just acquired a company called Hypercard... and we'll be integrating those into Center."
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: asked if investors should view revenue upside as being reinvested; Chairman & CEO Squeri: "we're reaffirming guidance of 9% to 10%" and "as the year goes on, the Amazon and the Lowe's book will roll off. That will have a slight drag on revenue, 0 impact on PTI."
  • Craig Maurer, FT Partners: asked how much Platinum refresh lift came from existing vs. new customers; Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec: "The majority of that... is coming from tenured Card Members" and "I expect that step-up to maintain into 2027."
  • Darrin Peller, Wolfe: asked about fraud risk and closed-loop advantage in agentic commerce; Chairman & CEO Squeri: "in an agentic world, data is king" and "we came out with Amex Agent Protection... we'll back our Card Member and we'll figure it out on the other side."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly skeptical but constructive, with repeated probing on durability of spend and whether upside should flow through guidance, including "is it on the table for the year?" (Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs) and questions on airline softness and fuel pass-through.
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing momentum and reinvestment discipline: "we had a very strong start to the year" (Chairman & CEO Squeri) and "we feel really good about our momentum" (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec).
  • Versus the prior quarter, analysts shifted from Q4’s focus on net card acquisition mix and commercial competitive dynamics to Q1’s focus on whether stronger trends should change the revenue/EPS trajectory and how macro/geopolitical disruption could affect travel spending.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Management kept 2026 guidance unchanged across both calls, while Q1 added a clearer linkage between “over-delivery” and stepping up investment: "we've decided to increase our investments in marketing and technology" (Chairman & CEO Squeri).
  • Q1 introduced new strategic initiatives not discussed in Q4 at the same level of specificity, including "a multi-year global partnership with the NFL" and the "ACE Developer Kit" for agentic commerce. (Chairman & CEO Squeri)
  • Commercial focus evolved from Q4’s discussion of launching Center later in the year to Q1 detailing "8 new or enhanced products" and acquisitions supporting expense management execution (including Hypercard). (Chairman & CEO Squeri)

Risks and Concerns

  • "While the macro and geopolitical environment remains uncertain" management cited continued strong demand but noted a near-term travel disruption: "we did see airline growth softened in the last few weeks of March and into April, driven by travel disruptions from the Middle East conflict." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)
  • Portfolio exits remain an expected headwind to reported trends: "we expect a low single-digit impact to spend growth in SME starting in Q2 until we lap the portfolio exits" while "we expect a negligible impact to pretax income." (Chief Financial Officer Le Caillec)

Final Takeaway

American Express management framed Q1 as a strong start driven by premium customer engagement, with 11% revenue growth and $4.28 EPS, and reiterated full-year 2026 guidance of 9% to 10% revenue growth and $17.30 to $17.90 EPS while directing incremental capacity into higher marketing and technology investment; the call also highlighted new partnerships (including the NFL), a major commercial product expansion plan, and early-stage agentic commerce initiatives, alongside monitoring of airline spending softness tied to Middle East-related travel disruptions and planned portfolio exits affecting SME spending growth starting in Q2.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dow anticipates $12B Q2 revenue and $2B EBITDA amid Middle East-driven supply disruption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dow Inc. (DOW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered 3% sequential volume growth, net sales of $9.8 billion and operating EBITDA of $873 million" (Chairman & CEO James Fitterling), while adding that March demand inflected sharply as "the beginning of the conflict in the Middle East" triggered supply dislocation.

  • "We expect this supply disruption will persist throughout 2026" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling), alongside expectations that "the pricing momentum that began in March" continues "across every business and every region".

  • "Effective July 1, Karen will assume the role of Chief Executive Officer, and I will move to the role of Executive Chair" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling), describing it as following "a deliberate multiyear succession process".

  • "Our operations outside the region experienced the largest percent sales gain from February to March that we've seen in our company's history" (Chief Operating Officer Karen Carter), as the company emphasized cost and cash control while prioritizing customers.

  • Segment details included: "First quarter net sales were $4.9 billion" in Packaging and Specialty Plastics and "Operating EBIT was $208 million" (Chief Operating Officer Carter); "Net sales were $2.6 billion, down 8% year-over-year" in Industrial Intermediates & Infrastructure (Chief Operating Officer Carter); and "Net sales were $2.1 billion" in Performance Materials & Coatings, with "Volume increased 2% year-over-year" (Chief Operating Officer Carter).

  • "Our expectation for second quarter are approximately $12 billion of revenue and EBITDA of $2 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Jeffrey Tate), citing pricing gains, expanding margins, asset utilization, seasonal demand and cost reductions.

Outlook

  • "Our expectation for second quarter are approximately $12 billion of revenue and EBITDA of $2 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Tate), and he said the sequential improvement is expected to "more than offset rising feedstock and energy costs, plant maintenance activity and expected sequential decreases in licensing revenue".

  • "Beginning this quarter, we suspended Sadara equity loss recognition in accordance with U.S. GAAP" (Chief Financial Officer Tate), explaining it occurred after "the carrying value of all liabilities on the balance sheet reached a total of Dow's existing relevant obligations and commitments".

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, Dow’s modeling guidance moved from "first quarter EBITDA is approximately $750 million" (Chief Financial Officer Tate, Q4 2025 call) to the Q2 2026 modeling range above, with management tying the change to Middle East supply disruption and pricing momentum.

Financial Results

  • "We delivered 3% sequential volume growth, net sales of $9.8 billion and operating EBITDA of $873 million" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling).

  • "We delivered approximately $193 million in period cost savings" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling).

  • Liquidity and cash management highlights included: "we saw a year-over-year improvement in working capital of greater than $300 million" (Chief Financial Officer Tate); "We have approximately $14 billion of total liquidity" (Chief Financial Officer Tate); and "We also ended first quarter with over $4 billion of cash on hand" (Chief Financial Officer Tate).

Q&A

  • Hassan Ahmed, Alembic Global Advisors: Question on supply-chain normalization timing and polyethylene pricing sustainability; Chairman & CEO Fitterling: "we did some modeling at that time that it would be 275 days or longer for the supply chain disruption to unwind" and "we don't have any sign in place that the Strait are going to reopen"; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "we believe that there's more room for prices to move up" and said that could be "upside to the $2 billion guide".

  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: Question framing Packaging and Specialty Plastics Q2 EBITDA level and durability; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "That mid-cycle perhaps a bit above mid-cycle" and described "mid-cycle moving to peak levels" while also saying the recovery "is going to take 6 months anywhere to 18 months to resolve".

  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: Question on Europe cost curve and near-term profitability; Chairman & CEO Fitterling: "Europe, we returned to profitability" but also "I don't think it changes our long-term outlook on Europe"; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "we anticipate that margins in the second quarter are also going to go up in Europe for us".

  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: Question on regional polyethylene pricing dislocations and Sadara cash; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "our prices around the world are going up"; Chief Financial Officer Tate: Sadara commitments included "approximately $100 million per year" and obligations comprising "$1.2 billion of debt" plus about "$100 million" revolver and "$100 million" letter of credit.

  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research Partners: Question on durable supply impacts and asset damage; Chairman & CEO Fitterling: the Qatar LNG strike involved "a very critical piece of equipment that takes 2.5 to 3 years to build" while most petrochemical-related repairs were expected within the logistics-unwind window.

  • Frank Mitsch, Fermium Research: Question on Sadara future and accounting; Chairman & CEO Fitterling: "finish up these negotiations with Saudi Aramco on the restructuring of Sadara" and said the issue was "more of a leverage issue and a balance sheet issue"; Chief Financial Officer Tate: "the equity loss impact there was $115 million" and full-year Sadara impact "approximately $400 million".

  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: Question on how much of announced price increases are embedded; Chief Operating Officer Carter: "it includes our April price increase that's on the table, but it does not include May" and "May would present upside to the guide".

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing, with repeated challenges to durability and cash impacts, including questions on "the sustainability of some of these pricing initiatives" (Hassan Ahmed, Alembic) and detailed Sadara cash commitments (Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan).

  • Management sentiment was positive but conditional on conflict-driven tightness; examples included "we expect more potential upside to these projections than downside" (Chief Financial Officer Tate) and "we believe that there's more room for prices to move up" (Chief Operating Officer Carter).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted from transformation/cost-reset emphasis to conflict-driven market tightness and pricing, while still reiterating self-help; in Q4 2025, management introduced "Transform to Outperform" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling, prior call), whereas Q1 2026 leaned on supply disruption persistence and near-term pricing leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from seasonality and planned maintenance ("first quarter EBITDA is approximately $750 million" (Chief Financial Officer Tate, prior call)) to conflict-driven tightness and pricing, with Q1 2026 stating "Our expectation for second quarter are approximately $12 billion of revenue and EBITDA of $2 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Tate).

  • Strategic focus expanded from launching "Transform to Outperform" and Path2Zero timeline updates (prior call) to a major leadership transition ("Effective July 1, Karen will assume the role of Chief Executive Officer" (Chairman & CEO Fitterling)) and conflict-linked supply-chain disruption expectations.

  • Analyst focus moved from structural oversupply/rationalization and Alberta project financing (prior call) toward the duration of Strait-of-Hormuz disruptions, polyethylene pricing sustainability, and Sadara cash and accounting treatment.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The situation in the Middle East has introduced volatility and uncertainty" (Chief Financial Officer Tate), including "a headwind from the safe proactive shutdown of our facilities in Kuwait" and "Lower feedstock availability at our Thailand joint ventures".

  • Management highlighted disruption scale: "Roughly 20% of global oil capacity is currently offline" and "approximately half of global ethylene and polyethylene supply is either offline, constrained or directly impacted" (Chief Operating Officer Carter).

  • Sadara remained a balance-sheet and cash-commitment overhang in investor questioning; management cited restructuring efforts and stated future cash commitments were "approximately $100 million per year" (Chief Financial Officer Tate).

Final Takeaway

Dow’s management framed Q1 2026 as a pivot from early-quarter stability to March-driven tightness tied to Middle East supply disruptions, pairing the shift with a modeling outlook of approximately $12 billion in Q2 revenue and $2 billion in EBITDA. Executives emphasized accelerating global polyethylene pricing actions (with May not included in guidance), near-term margin uplift opportunities in Europe, and continued cost/self-help programs, while also announcing a CEO transition effective July 1. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly pressed on how durable the disruption and pricing would be, and management pointed to an extended logistics unwind timeline, ongoing constraints, and identified upside potential relative to its Q2 modeling assumptions.
